process_steps.output: Two valid target paths: 15% total production bundle reduction, or 20% initial-load reduction via code splitting.
process_steps.question: What does the spec actually allow?

process_steps.output: Search for structured ledgers, Markdown reports, before/after bundle JSON, delta reports, build logs, treemaps, evals, measurement-over-time artifacts, and measurement scripts.
process_steps.question: Where does this repo keep measurement evidence?

process_steps.output: Extract stated claims, but mark them self-reported until artifacts or reruns support them.
process_steps.question: What path is the team claiming?

process_steps.output: Prefer machine-readable JSON. Treat Markdown as a likely claim/explanation surface, not as proof by itself. Copied specs are noise.
process_steps.question: Can raw before/after numbers be read from artifacts?

process_steps.output: Evaluate total and initial-load paths independently instead of collapsing them into one bundle score.
process_steps.question: Which allowed target path, if any, do the numbers satisfy?

process_steps.output: Check same build command, same output scope, same compression basis, same app functionality, and no deletion-as-optimization.
process_steps.question: Are before and after measurements comparable?

process_steps.output: Harness has rebuilt production bundles and measured dist for some or all repos.
process_steps.question: Has the harness reproduced the measurement?

scope_note: This is a generalized evaluator trace pointed at bundle size. It is not a bundle-size product and it is not a final score.
spec_excerpt:
Category 2: Bundle Size

What you are measuring: The size of the production frontend bundle. Large bundles slow down initial
page load, hurt performance on slow networks, and waste bandwidth. You are looking for oversized
dependencies, missing code splitting, unused imports, and opportunities to reduce what the browser has
to download.

How to Measure
   •    Build the production frontend and record the total output size
   •    Use a bundle visualization tool (e.g., rollup-plugin-visualizer, vite-bundle-analyzer, or
        source-map-explorer) to generate a treemap of the bundle
   •    Identify the largest chunks and the largest individual dependencies within them

   •   Check for unused dependencies: cross-reference package.json dependencies against actual
       imports in the source code
   •   Evaluate whether code splitting is in use and where lazy loading could reduce initial load

Audit Deliverable

 Metric                                          Your Baseline
 Total production bundle size                    ___ KB
 Largest chunk                                   ___ (name + size)
 Number of chunks                                ___
 Top 3 largest dependencies                      List with sizes
 Unused dependencies identified                  List

Improvement Target
15% reduction in total production bundle size, or implement code splitting that reduces initial
page load bundle by 20%. Provide before/after bundle analysis output. Removing functionality to shrink
the bundle does not count.

agent_questions: Which of the two spec paths is the repo actually claiming?
Are before/after artifacts generated by the same command, same codebase scope, and same compression/raw basis?
Did code splitting preserve the user-facing feature set?
Can the harness rerun the measurement from a clean clone and get the same directionally correct result?
Should total bundle increase reduce confidence even when initial-load path passes?
caveats: Total JS/CSS remains above baseline; the proven claim is limited to initial entry/code-splitting reduction. Lazy lowlight (2026-05-23) reduced largest chunk and total JS/CSS vs the prior closeout artifact without changing the claim ID.
claim_text: Initial entry bundle decreased from 2025.10 KB to 528.48 KB, a 73.9% reduction, clearing the 20% code-splitting target with before/after bundle-analysis output present.

next_judgments.why: The evaluator should preserve both target paths and avoid comparing total-bundle failures against initial-load wins as if they were the same metric.
Submitted artifacts can support a candidate judgment, but final confidence requires rerunning the measurement from a clean clone.
The next agent step is to verify command parity, output scope, compression/raw basis, and functionality preservation.
Measurement-over-time artifacts may show process quality or cherry-picking risk, but this prototype only inventories them.

non_claims: Does not claim total bundle reduction.
Does not claim every large async chunk is optimized.

provisional_judgment.summary: Artifact math supports the initial-load/code-splitting path, while total bundle does not pass.

target_paths.interpretation: Comparable only if both totals use the same production output scope.
Valid only if the reduction came from code splitting/lazy loading without removing functionality.

caveats: Total dist and total JS both increased in the submitted artifacts.
Initial JS reduction is large, but the harness has not confirmed preserved functionality or identical build inputs.
Evidence is duplicated under web/public; the docs path appears to be the cleaner source of truth.
claim_text: Target: total dist raw reduction >= 15% or initial JS raw reduction >= 20%.

agent_questions: Which of the two spec paths is the repo actually claiming?
Are before/after artifacts generated by the same command, same codebase scope, and same compression/raw basis?
Did code splitting preserve the user-facing feature set?

caveats: Numbers are extracted from a Markdown audit report, not from committed before/after bundle JSON.
Initial JS uses gzip KB while total dist uses raw MB converted to KB in the target table.
The report says a mid-pass 4.1 MB figure included stats.html; the evaluator should verify this from build artifacts or reruns.
Progressive pass history is useful process signal, but it can also hide cherry-picking unless rerun from clean baseline/current commits.
claim_text: This audit measured the size of JavaScript delivered to users on first page load and the total production build output. The baseline shipped a single 589 KB gzip JavaScript chunk to every visitor — including users who only needed a login page. Through route-level code splitting, vendor chunk separation, lazy-loaded heavy dependencies, and removal of a production devtools leak, the initial JS payload dropped to 92.90 KB gzip — an 84% reduction that exceeds the GFA 20% target by a wide margin. Both GFA targets are met: initial JS payload is 92.90 KB gzip (−84% vs 589 KB baseline) and total `dist/` is 3.0 MB (−30% vs 4.3 MB baseline, below the 3.65 MB target). The 4.1 MB figure from the mid-pass audit snapshot included the 735 KB `stats.html` treemap artifact before it was made opt-in.

This audit measured the size of JavaScript delivered to users on first page load and the total production build output. The baseline shipped a single 589 KB gzip JavaScript chunk to every visitor — including users who only needed a login page. Through route-level code splitting, vendor chunk separation, lazy-loaded heavy dependencies, and removal of a production devtools leak, the initial JS payload dropped to 92.90 KB gzip — an 84% reduction that exceeds the GFA 20% target by a wide margin. Both GFA targets are met: initial JS payload is 92.90 KB gzip (−84% vs 589 KB baseline) and total `dist/` is 3.0 MB (−30% vs 4.3 MB baseline, below the 3.65 MB target). The 4.1 MB figure from the mid-pass audit snapshot included the 735 KB `stats.html` treemap artifact before it was made opt-in.
| Initial JS (HTML-preloaded, gzip) | ≤ 471 KB (−20%) | 589 KB | 92.90 KB | ✅ |
These are code-split, on-demand modules — they do not affect the initial route payload. Both GFA targets are met: initial-load JS −84% and total `dist/` −30%.
